title: Grid Search: Testing Every Combo
concept: Systematically testing every combination of hyperparameters from lists of options, similar to scanning a Battleship grid.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a risk of setting a Decision Tree's max depth knob too high?

options.text: The model will overfit by memorizing training noise instead of general patterns.
The model will underfit and be too simple.
The model will delete its own parameters.

exercises.explain: Correct! High depth limits allow trees to grow complex and overfit by memorizing noisy details.

cards.title: Playing Battleship
cards.body: In the game Battleship, you look for hidden ships by calling out grid coordinates. If you want to be completely thorough, you might call out every single cell in a small area: A1, A2, A3, B1, B2, B3... until you hit a target. Computers do the exact same thing to find the best settings!

cards.title: What is Grid Search?
cards.body: Grid Search is a systematic method for finding the best hyperparameters. You make a list of options for each knob. The computer then builds and tests a model for every possible combination of those options to find the winner.

cards.title: The search grid
cards.body: If you want to tune two knobs: max depth [3, 5, 7] (3 options) and minimum samples [2, 4, 6] (3 options), you can arrange them like a coordinate grid. Testing every combination forms a 3x3 grid, meaning you will train and compare 9 different models.

cards.title: Counting combinations
cards.body:
Let's count the models to train:
• Knob 1 (Max Depth): [2, 5] (2 options)
• Knob 2 (k-Neighbors): [3, 5, 7] (3 options)
Grid search will multiply these options: 2 * 3 = 6 total combinations to test.

cards.title: Guaranteed but limited
cards.body:
Myth: Grid Search guarantees finding the absolute best possible settings for your model.
Reality: Grid Search only tests the specific numbers on your lists. If the absolute best setting is max_depth = 4, but you only listed 3 and 5, Grid Search will completely miss it!

cards.title: Grid Search in action
cards.body: How options combine into a grid of test models:

left.text:
Max Depth: [3, 5]
Min Leaf: [2, 4]
You specify these lists.

exercises.prompt: If you use Grid Search to test 3 options for max depth and 4 options for minimum samples, how many total models will you train?
exercises.explain: Correct! Grid Search multiplies the choices: 3 options × 4 options = 12 total combinations to test.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Grid Search will automatically test numbers not on your list, such as a depth of 4 if your list contains only [3, 5].
exercises.explain: False! Grid Search only tests the exact values you provide on your lists. It skips everything else.

exercises.sentence: Because Grid Search tests every possible combination, it can become very ___ if you test many hyperparameters.
exercises.explain: Correct! The number of models multiplies with each new choice, making Grid Search slow and resource-heavy.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes a Grid Search setup?

options.text: Testing all combinations of max depth [3, 5] and neighbors [2, 4, 6].
Testing randomly chosen numbers until time runs out.
Letting the model adjust its parameters during training.

exercises.explain: Defining lists of options and systematically testing every single combination is Grid Search.

exercises.prompt: You want to find the best settings for a complex model. What is the main drawback of using Grid Search that you should prepare for?

options.text: It can take a very long time because the number of combinations multiplies rapidly with each new setting.
It randomly skips combinations, which might miss the best setting.
It only works on small datasets with fewer than 10 rows.
It changes the training data labels automatically.

exercises.explain: Perfect! Since the options multiply, testing 4 knobs with 5 choices each requires training 625 models, which gets very slow. Next, let's look at a faster alternative!
